expanded_description:
Expansionary monetary policy (also called easy money policy or accommodative policy) is used by central banks to combat recessions, stimulate economic growth, and prevent deflation. When the economy is contracting—GDP falling, unemployment rising, inflation below target—the central bank acts to lower interest rates, increase the money supply, and encourage borrowing and spending. The goal is to close the output gap (bring actual GDP back to potential) and return inflation to target.

The primary tools of expansionary policy are: lowering the policy interest rate (federal funds rate in the US), conducting open market purchases of securities (including quantitative easing when rates are at zero), lowering the discount rate, reducing reserve requirements (if still used), and providing forward guidance about future policy. In normal times, the central bank lowers short-term interest rates. When rates hit the zero lower bound (ZLB), the central bank must turn to unconventional tools: quantitative easing (QE), forward guidance, and negative interest rates (in some jurisdictions).

Understanding expansionary monetary policy is essential for analyzing how central banks respond to recessions, evaluating the effectiveness of policy in different economic environments (liquidity trap vs. normal times), and assessing the risks of policy (inflation, asset bubbles, financial instability). In this section, we will examine the transmission mechanism of expansionary policy, the conventional and unconventional tools used at the zero lower bound, the effectiveness of policy in the 2008 crisis and 2020 pandemic, the risks of keeping rates too low for too long, and the challenges of exiting from expansionary policy (tapering, quantitative tightening). You will learn to analyze the effects of rate cuts on the economy, evaluate the effectiveness of QE, and understand the policy trade-offs involved in fighting recessions.

core_concept.principle:
Expansionary monetary policy aims to stimulate economic activity by lowering interest rates, increasing the money supply, and encouraging borrowing and spending. The policy works through several channels:

Conventional Expansionary Tools:
• Lower the policy interest rate (federal funds rate): Reduces the cost of borrowing for banks, which passes through to households and firms.
• Open market purchases (bond buying): Injects reserves into the banking system, increasing the money supply and lowering interest rates.
• Lower the discount rate: Encourages banks to borrow reserves from the central bank, increasing liquidity.
• Lower reserve requirements: Frees up reserves for lending, increasing the money multiplier (rarely used).
• Forward guidance: Communicates that rates will stay low for an extended period, lowering long-term rates.

Unconventional Tools (at the Zero Lower Bound):
• Quantitative easing (QE): Large-scale purchases of long-term bonds (Treasuries, MBS) to lower long-term interest rates.
• Negative interest rates: Pushing policy rates below zero to penalize banks for holding excess reserves (ECB, BOJ).
• Yield curve control (YCC): Capping long-term bond yields by committing to buy unlimited bonds at a target yield (BOJ).
• Credit easing: Purchasing private sector assets (corporate bonds, commercial paper) to lower credit spreads.

Transmission Channels:
• Interest rate channel: Lower rates reduce borrowing costs for consumption (housing, autos) and investment.
• Credit channel: Lower rates ease bank lending standards and increase credit availability.
• Asset price channel: Lower rates raise stock and real estate prices, increasing wealth and spending (wealth effect).
• Exchange rate channel: Lower rates depreciate the currency, boosting net exports.
• Expectations channel: Forward guidance lowers expected future rates, reducing long-term yields.

Key Relationships:
• $i \downarrow \rightarrow I \uparrow, C \uparrow, NX \uparrow \rightarrow AD \uparrow \rightarrow Y \uparrow, \pi \uparrow$
• In a liquidity trap (i near zero), conventional policy is ineffective; unconventional tools (QE, forward guidance) are needed.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the economy is in a recession: GDP has fallen for two quarters, unemployment has risen to 8%, and inflation has dropped to 0.5% (below the 2% target). The Fed decides to implement expansionary monetary policy.

First, the Fed lowers the federal funds rate target from 5% to 4%. Banks lower their prime rate (the rate they charge their best customers) from 8% to 7%. A business considering a new factory can now borrow at a lower rate, making the investment more profitable. A family considering buying a house sees mortgage rates fall from 6% to 5%, making the monthly payment more affordable. They buy the house, stimulating construction and related industries.

The Fed also conducts open market purchases, buying Treasury bonds from banks. Banks receive new reserves, which they lend out. The money supply expands, putting further downward pressure on interest rates. Stock prices rise (lower discount rates increase the present value of future earnings), increasing household wealth. Households feel richer and spend more (the wealth effect). The lower interest rate also causes the dollar to depreciate (investors seek higher yields elsewhere), making US exports cheaper and imports more expensive, boosting net exports.

If the recession is severe and the Fed has already cut rates to zero (the zero lower bound), it cannot cut further. It turns to unconventional tools: quantitative easing (buying long-term bonds to lower long-term rates directly), forward guidance (promising to keep rates at zero for years), and possibly credit easing (buying corporate bonds to lower credit spreads). These tools provide additional stimulus when conventional tools are exhausted.

image.alt: Graph: AD-AS model showing recession (AD left of LRAS); expansionary policy shifts AD right, closing output gap; lower interest rate shown in money market graph.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.1: Expansionary monetary policy shifts aggregate demand right, closing the output gap and returning the economy to potential output.

sections.introduction:
Expansionary monetary policy (also called easy money policy or accommodative policy) is used by central banks to combat recessions, stimulate economic growth, and prevent deflation. When the economy is contracting—GDP falling, unemployment rising, inflation below target—the central bank acts to lower interest rates, increase the money supply, and encourage borrowing and spending. The goal is to close the output gap (bring actual GDP back to potential) and return inflation to target.

The primary tools of expansionary policy are: lowering the policy interest rate (federal funds rate in the US), conducting open market purchases of securities (including quantitative easing when rates are at zero), lowering the discount rate, reducing reserve requirements (if still used), and providing forward guidance about future policy. In normal times, the central bank lowers short-term interest rates. When rates hit the zero lower bound (ZLB), the central bank must turn to unconventional tools: quantitative easing (QE), forward guidance, and negative interest rates (in some jurisdictions).

In this section, we will examine the transmission mechanism of expansionary policy, the conventional and unconventional tools used at the zero lower bound, the effectiveness of policy in the 2008 crisis and 2020 pandemic, the risks of keeping rates too low for too long, and the challenges of exiting from expansionary policy (tapering, quantitative tightening).

introduction_image.alt: Timeline: 2001 recession (Fed cuts from 6.5% to 1%); 2008 crisis (cuts to zero, QE1, QE2, QE3); 2020 pandemic (cuts to zero, unlimited QE); 2024 (normalization).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 8.3.2: Historical episodes of expansionary monetary policy: the Fed cuts rates aggressively during recessions and uses QE when rates hit zero.

key_concept.definition:
Expansionary monetary policy is policy designed to increase aggregate demand, lower unemployment, and prevent deflation by lowering interest rates, increasing the money supply, and easing credit conditions.

Conventional Tools:
• Lower policy interest rate: Reduces the federal funds rate target, lowering borrowing costs throughout the economy.
• Open market purchases: Buying government securities injects reserves, increasing the money supply and lowering rates.
• Lower discount rate: Reduces the cost of borrowing from the discount window, encouraging banks to borrow.
• Lower reserve requirements: Frees reserves for lending, increasing the money multiplier (rarely used).
• Forward guidance: Communicating that rates will stay low for an extended period to lower long-term rates.

Unconventional Tools (Zero Lower Bound):
• Quantitative easing (QE): Large-scale purchases of long-term securities (Treasuries, MBS) to lower long-term yields.
• Negative interest rates: Setting policy rates below zero to penalize excess reserves (ECB, BOJ, SNB).
• Yield curve control (YCC): Committing to buy unlimited bonds at a target yield to cap long-term rates.
• Credit easing: Purchasing private sector assets (corporate bonds, commercial paper) to lower credit spreads.

Transmission Channels:
• Interest rate channel: Lower rates reduce cost of capital for investment and durable goods.
• Credit channel: Lower rates ease lending standards and increase credit availability.
• Asset price channel: Lower rates raise asset prices (stocks, real estate), increasing wealth and spending.
• Exchange rate channel: Lower rates depreciate the currency, boosting net exports.
• Expectations channel: Forward guidance lowers expected future rates, reducing long-term yields.

Policy Objective:
Close the output gap ($Y < Y^*$), return inflation to target ($\pi = \pi^*$), and reduce unemployment to the natural rate ($u = u^*$).
key_concept.context: The Fed has used expansionary policy in every recession since the 1970s. The 1990-1991 recession saw rates cut from 8% to 3%. The 2001 recession (dot-com bust, 9/11) saw rates cut from 6.5% to 1%. The 2008 financial crisis saw rates cut to zero for the first time, followed by QE. The 2020 pandemic saw rates cut to zero again, with unlimited QE. Each episode taught lessons: (1) aggressive cuts work faster than gradual cuts; (2) the zero lower bound requires unconventional tools; (3) forward guidance enhances policy effectiveness; (4) exit must be managed carefully to avoid market disruption. The debate over expansionary policy centers on risks: inflation, asset bubbles, financial instability, and the difficulty of exiting (taper tantrum 2013).

image.alt: Flowchart: Fed lowers policy rate → lower short-term rates → lower long-term rates, higher asset prices, weaker currency → higher investment, consumption, net exports → higher AD → higher output and inflation.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.3: The transmission mechanism of expansionary monetary policy: from policy action to economic outcomes.

classifications.type: Conventional Expansionary Policy: Interest Rate Cuts
classifications.value: Lowering the policy interest rate is the primary conventional tool for stimulating the economy
characteristics: Fed lowers federal funds rate target → banks lower prime rate → borrowing costs fall
Effect on investment: Lower cost of capital increases business investment (machinery, structures, R&D)
Effect on consumption: Lower rates reduce payments on variable-rate debt; lower mortgage rates boost housing
Effect on net exports: Lower rates depreciate currency, boosting exports, reducing imports
Effect on asset prices: Lower discount rates raise stock and real estate prices (wealth effect)
Lags: Full effect on output takes 6-12 months; on inflation takes 12-24 months
classifications.behavior:
When the Fed lowers the federal funds rate, the effects cascade through the economy. The prime rate (the rate banks charge their best customers) typically moves one-for-one with the fed funds rate. Mortgage rates, auto loan rates, and corporate bond yields move less than one-for-one but still significantly. Lower borrowing costs make large purchases (houses, cars, machinery) more affordable, increasing demand.

The wealth effect is important: lower interest rates increase the present value of future earnings, raising stock prices. Higher stock prices make households feel wealthier, increasing consumption (marginal propensity to consume out of wealth is about 3-5%). Lower mortgage rates also increase housing wealth (higher home prices), further boosting consumption.

The exchange rate channel: lower US interest rates make dollar-denominated assets less attractive to foreign investors, causing the dollar to depreciate. A weaker dollar makes US exports cheaper abroad and imports more expensive at home, boosting net exports.

The effectiveness of rate cuts depends on the state of the economy. In a normal recession, rate cuts work well. But in a severe recession or financial crisis, banks may be unwilling to lend (credit crunch), and households may be unwilling to borrow (debt overhang), blunting the transmission. This is why the Fed also uses credit easing (direct lending to non-banks) during crises.

image.alt: Graph: Fed funds rate cuts (2007-2009) and subsequent recovery in GDP, housing starts, auto sales; lags shown.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.4: Conventional rate cuts stimulate the economy through multiple channels, but with variable lags.

examples.explanation: From January 2001 to June 2003, the Fed cut the federal funds rate from 6.5% to 1% (550 basis points). Mortgage rates fell from 8% to 5.5%. Housing starts rose from 1.2 million to 1.8 million. Auto sales surged with 0% financing offers. The economy recovered, with GDP growth reaching 4% by 2004. However, critics argue rates stayed too low for too long, contributing to the housing bubble.

In July-October 2019, the Fed cut rates from 2.50% to 1.75% (75 basis points) in response to trade war uncertainty and global slowdown. Mortgage rates fell from 4.5% to 3.7%, boosting housing. The economy avoided recession, and unemployment continued to fall. This was a preemptive expansionary move rather than a response to an existing recession.

classifications.value: When the policy rate hits the zero lower bound, central banks use QE to lower long-term interest rates directly
characteristics: QE: Large-scale purchases of long-term government bonds and mortgage-backed securities (MBS)
Goal: Lower long-term interest rates (10-year Treasury yield) to stimulate housing and investment
Channel 1 (portfolio balance): QE removes duration from the market, reducing term premiums
Channel 2 (signaling): QE signals that the Fed will keep rates low for a long time
Channel 3 (liquidity): QE improves market functioning during crises
classifications.behavior:
Quantitative easing (QE) is the purchase of long-term securities by the central bank, typically when short-term rates are already at zero. The Fed buys Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities (MBS) from banks and investors, paying with reserves. This increases the demand for these securities, pushing their prices up and yields down.

QE works through three channels:
1. Portfolio balance channel: The Fed removes long-term bonds from private portfolios, forcing investors to buy other assets (stocks, corporate bonds), lowering yields across the board.
2. Signaling channel: QE signals that the Fed will keep short-term rates low for a long time, lowering expected future short rates and thus long-term yields.
3. Liquidity channel: In a crisis, QE provides liquidity to dysfunctional markets (e.g., MBS market in 2008, corporate bond market in 2020).

Research suggests that the Fed's QE programs lowered the 10-year Treasury yield by 1-1.5 percentage points. QE also boosted stock prices and housing wealth. However, QE has diminishing returns: each additional \$100 billion of purchases has a smaller effect. QE also carries risks: (1) inflation (though this did not materialize in 2009-2019); (2) asset bubbles; (3) difficulty exiting (taper tantrum 2013).

image.alt: Graph: Fed balance sheet size (trillions) and 10-year Treasury yield (2008-2024); QE episodes marked; yields fall during QE, rise during taper.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.5: Quantitative easing expands the Fed's balance sheet and lowers long-term interest rates; the effect is strongest when QE is announced.

examples.explanation: In November 2008, the Fed announced it would buy \$600 billion of MBS and agency debt. Mortgage rates fell from 6.5% to 5.0% within months. The housing market stabilized. QE1 is credited with preventing a deeper collapse, though its effect on growth was modest (estimated 0.5-1.0% GDP boost).

In March 2020, the Fed announced unlimited QE, buying \$120 billion per month (\$80B Treasuries, \$40B MBS). The 10-year Treasury yield fell from 1.5% to 0.5%. Mortgage rates fell from 3.5% to 2.7%, fueling a housing boom. QE4, combined with fiscal stimulus, helped the economy recover rapidly. The Fed began tapering in November 2021 and ended purchases in March 2022.

classifications.value: Forward guidance is communication about the likely future path of policy; it lowers long-term rates even when short rates are at zero
characteristics: Calendar-based guidance: 'Rates will stay low until a specific date' (e.g., 'at least through mid-2024')
State-based guidance: 'Rates will stay low until economic conditions are met' (e.g., 'until inflation averages 2%')
Open-ended guidance: 'Rates will stay low for a considerable period' (vague, less effective)
Forward guidance lowers long-term rates by reducing expected future short rates
Particularly important at the zero lower bound, where the Fed cannot cut further
Credibility is crucial: if markets doubt the Fed's commitment, guidance is ineffective
classifications.behavior:
Forward guidance is a powerful tool, especially when the policy rate is at the zero lower bound. By committing to keep rates low for an extended period, the Fed lowers market expectations of future short-term rates, which reduces long-term yields (since long-term yields are averages of expected future short rates plus term premiums).

The Fed has used various forms of forward guidance:
• Open-ended (2003-2004): 'Accommodation can be removed at a pace that is likely to be measured'—vague, not very effective.
• Calendar-based (2011-2012): 'Rates will stay low at least through mid-2013'—more effective but markets questioned commitment.
• State-based (2012-2014): 'Rates will stay low until unemployment falls below 6.5%'—more credible because tied to outcomes.
• Average inflation targeting (2020-present): 'Rates will stay low until inflation averages 2% over time'—designed to prevent premature tightening.

Forward guidance is most effective when it is (1) clear, (2) credible, and (3) tied to observable outcomes. The Fed's 2012 Evans Rule (unemployment >6.5% and inflation <2.5%) was highly effective because markets could verify conditions. The 2020 AIT framework is more complex but has been effective in anchoring expectations.

image.alt: Graph: Market-implied path of federal funds rate before and after forward guidance; guidance lowers expected future rates, reducing long-term yields.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.6: Forward guidance lowers market expectations of future short-term rates, reducing long-term yields and providing stimulus.

examples.explanation: In December 2012, the Fed announced it would keep rates near zero at least until unemployment fell below 6.5% (provided inflation remained below 2.5%). This state-based guidance was credible because it tied policy to observable outcomes. Markets pushed out expectations of rate hikes, lowering long-term yields by 0.3-0.5 percentage points.

In August 2020, the Fed announced a new framework: it would allow inflation to run moderately above 2% for some time to average 2% over time. This signaled that the Fed would not raise rates preemptively, even if inflation temporarily exceeded 2%. The guidance helped keep long-term rates low during the 2021-2022 inflation surge (though inflation proved much higher than expected, forcing the Fed to tighten earlier than the guidance suggested).

classifications.type: Risks and Limitations of Expansionary Policy
classifications.value: Expansionary policy carries risks including inflation, asset bubbles, financial instability, and exit challenges
characteristics: Inflation risk: Too much stimulus can cause overheating and high inflation (1970s, 2021-2022)
Asset bubbles: Low rates can fuel speculation in stocks, housing, crypto (dot-com 2000, housing 2006, meme stocks 2021)
Financial instability: Low rates encourage risk-taking (search for yield), leading to excessive leverage
Currency depreciation: Aggressive easing can trigger currency wars (2010-2012)
Exit challenges: Tightening too late risks inflation; tightening too early risks double-dip recession
Taper tantrum (2013): Markets reacted violently when Fed hinted at reducing QE
classifications.behavior:
Expansionary monetary policy is not without risks. The most obvious is inflation: if the Fed stimulates too much or for too long, aggregate demand can outpace supply, causing prices to rise. This happened in the 1970s (Fed kept rates too low for too long) and again in 2021-2022 (pandemic stimulus combined with supply shocks).

Asset bubbles are another risk. Low interest rates encourage investors to search for yield, driving up prices of risky assets. The dot-com bubble (1990s) and housing bubble (2000s) were both fueled by low rates. Some argue that the Fed's post-2008 low rates contributed to the meme stock and crypto bubbles of 2020-2021.

Financial instability can arise when low rates encourage excessive leverage and risk-taking. Banks may lend too much; investors may borrow to buy risky assets. When rates eventually rise, these positions can unwind rapidly, causing a crisis (e.g., Silicon Valley Bank 2023, which failed due to interest rate risk).

Exit challenges are the most difficult aspect of expansionary policy. The Fed must eventually tighten to prevent inflation, but tightening too early can choke off the recovery (1937 double-dip recession). Tightening too late risks high inflation (1970s). The 2013 'taper tantrum' showed how sensitive markets are to exit signals: when then-Chair Bernanke hinted that the Fed might reduce QE, long-term yields spiked 1% in weeks, threatening the recovery. The Fed learned to communicate exit more carefully, using forward guidance to prepare markets.

image.alt: Diagram: Expansionary policy risks (inflation, asset bubbles, financial instability, exit challenges) with historical examples (1970s, 2000s, 2013, 2023).
image.caption: Figure 8.3.7: Expansionary monetary policy carries significant risks; managing the exit is as important as the initial stimulus.

examples.explanation: In May 2013, Fed Chair Bernanke testified that the Fed might 'reduce the pace of asset purchases' in coming meetings. Markets reacted violently: the 10-year Treasury yield rose from 1.6% to 2.6% in two months. Mortgage rates spiked, and emerging markets saw capital outflows. The Fed delayed tapering until December 2013, and when it finally tapered, it communicated more carefully. The taper tantrum taught the Fed that exit must be telegraphed well in advance.

SVB failed because it had invested heavily in long-term Treasury bonds during the low-rate environment of 2020-2021. When the Fed raised rates aggressively in 2022-2023, the value of those bonds collapsed, causing a \$2 billion loss. A bank run ensued. This is an example of financial instability caused by expansionary policy (low rates encouraged yield-seeking) followed by rapid tightening.

principles.principle: Expansionary Monetary Policy is More Effective When Combined with Fiscal Policy, Especially at the Zero Lower Bound
principles.statement: At the zero lower bound, monetary policy faces diminishing returns. The Fed can lower rates to zero, but it cannot go negative (or only slightly negative). QE and forward guidance provide additional stimulus, but their effects are limited. In a deep recession or liquidity trap, fiscal policy (government spending, tax cuts, transfers) is more effective because it puts money directly into the hands of spenders. The 2008 crisis and 2020 pandemic both demonstrated the power of fiscal-monetary coordination: the Fed provided low rates and liquidity, while Congress provided stimulus checks, unemployment benefits, and infrastructure spending. The combined effect was much larger than either policy alone. The multiplier for fiscal policy is larger at the ZLB (1.5-2.0) than in normal times (0.5-1.0) because monetary policy does not crowd out private spending (rates cannot rise).
principles.application: Policymakers should coordinate monetary and fiscal policy during severe recessions. The Fed should lower rates to zero and provide liquidity; Congress should enact fiscal stimulus (spending increases or tax cuts). The Fed can also finance fiscal stimulus by purchasing government debt (monetization), though this raises inflation risks. The 2020 CARES Act and American Rescue Plan, combined with Fed QE, produced the fastest recovery on record, though it also produced the highest inflation in 40 years. The lesson is that coordination works, but the scale matters: too much stimulus can cause overheating.
principles.example: In 2009, the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (\$800 billion fiscal stimulus) combined with Fed rate cuts (to zero) and QE1. The economy bottomed in mid-2009 and grew slowly. In 2020, the CARES Act (\$2.2 trillion) and American Rescue Plan (\$1.9 trillion) combined with Fed rate cuts (to zero) and unlimited QE. The economy recovered much faster (V-shaped), but inflation surged to 9% in 2022. The difference was the scale of fiscal stimulus: 2020-2021 stimulus was much larger relative to the output gap, causing overheating.

image.alt: Graph: GDP growth during 2008 recession (slow recovery) vs. 2020 recession (fast recovery); faster recovery in 2020 attributed to larger fiscal stimulus.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.8: The 2020 recovery was faster than the 2008 recovery due to larger fiscal stimulus coordinated with monetary easing.

principles.principle: The Effectiveness of Expansionary Policy Diminishes at the Zero Lower Bound; Unconventional Tools Have Diminishing Returns
principles.statement: When the policy rate hits zero, the marginal effectiveness of additional easing declines. The first 100 basis points of rate cuts have a large effect on borrowing costs and asset prices. The 100th basis point (from 1% to 0%) has a smaller effect because rates are already low. Similarly, QE has diminishing returns: the first \$500 billion of purchases lowers long-term yields by 0.5-1.0 percentage points; the next \$500 billion has a smaller effect (0.2-0.3 percentage points). This is because the portfolio balance channel becomes saturated: once the Fed has removed most long-term bonds from private portfolios, additional purchases have little effect. Forward guidance also has diminishing returns: once markets already expect rates to stay low for years, additional guidance has little effect. This is why central banks must use a combination of tools at the ZLB, and why fiscal policy becomes relatively more important as monetary policy reaches its limits.
principles.application: Central banks should use the most effective tools first: aggressive rate cuts, then QE, then forward guidance, then negative rates (if needed). They should recognize that the marginal benefit of additional easing declines. This implies that there is an optimal scale for QE: enough to lower long-term rates significantly, but not so much that the costs (inflation risk, exit difficulty) outweigh the benefits. The Fed's QE programs (\$3.5 trillion total from 2008-2014) were likely near the optimum; the 2020-2022 QE (\$4 trillion in two years) may have been excessive, contributing to inflation.
principles.example: During QE1 (2008-2010), the Fed purchased \$1.75 trillion in securities. Research estimates this lowered the 10-year Treasury yield by 1.0-1.5 percentage points. During QE2 (2010-2011), the Fed purchased \$600 billion; the estimated effect was 0.2-0.3 percentage points. During QE3 (2012-2014), the Fed purchased \$85 billion per month; the marginal effect was even smaller. The diminishing returns were clear: each subsequent QE round had less impact on long-term yields, even though the Fed was buying similar amounts. This is why the Fed ended QE3 in 2014 despite still having a large balance sheet.

image.alt: Graph: Marginal effect of QE on 10-year yield as function of cumulative purchases; decreasing slope shows diminishing returns.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.9: The effectiveness of QE diminishes with scale; early purchases have large effects, later purchases have smaller marginal effects.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Effect of Rate Cuts on Mortgage Payments and Housing Demand

image.alt: Table: Fed funds rate cut from 5% to 3%; mortgage rate falls from 6.5% to 4.5%; monthly payment on $300k mortgage falls from $1,896 to $1,520; payment reduction 20%.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.10: Worked example: calculating the impact of expansionary rate cuts on mortgage affordability.

worked_examples.problem:
The Fed cuts the federal funds rate from 5.00% to 3.00% to combat a recession. Mortgage rates typically move 60% of the change in the fed funds rate (pass-through coefficient = 0.6). The 30-year fixed mortgage rate was initially 6.50%.

a) Calculate the new mortgage rate after the Fed's rate cut.
b) For a \$300,000 mortgage, calculate the monthly payment before and after the rate cut.
c) Calculate the percentage reduction in the monthly payment.
d) If the price elasticity of housing demand is -1.0, what is the expected percentage increase in home sales?
e) Why might the actual increase in sales be smaller than the elasticity predicts?

step_by_step.explanation:
Fed funds rate cut = 5.00% - 3.00% = 2.00 percentage points.

Pass-through coefficient = 0.6, so mortgage rate change = 2.00% × 0.6 = 1.20 percentage points.

New mortgage rate = 6.50% - 1.20% = 5.30%

Percentage reduction = $(1,898 - 1,669) / 1,898 = 229 / 1,898 = 12.1\%$

A 1.20 percentage point reduction in mortgage rates (from 6.50% to 5.30%) reduced the monthly payment by 12.1%.

step_by_step.step: Calculate expected increase in home sales
step_by_step.explanation:
Price elasticity of housing demand = -1.0. This means a 1% decrease in the effective price (user cost) leads to a 1% increase in quantity demanded.

The monthly payment is a proxy for the user cost. A 12.1% reduction in payment is a 12.1% reduction in user cost.

Expected increase in home sales = 12.1% × 1.0 = 12.1%

step_by_step.step: Explain why actual increase may be smaller
step_by_step.explanation:
The actual increase in home sales may be smaller than the elasticity predicts for several reasons:
1. Credit conditions: During a recession, banks tighten lending standards. Even if rates are lower, some borrowers cannot qualify for loans.
2. Uncertainty: Households may delay home purchases due to job insecurity or economic uncertainty, despite lower rates.
3. Negative equity: Some homeowners owe more than their home is worth (underwater), preventing them from moving.
4. Supply constraints: If housing supply is inelastic (limited land, construction bottlenecks), sales may not increase as much as demand.
5. Elasticity estimates: The -1.0 elasticity is a long-run estimate; short-run elasticity is smaller (households adjust slowly).
6. Other costs: Property taxes, insurance, and maintenance may not fall with rates, reducing the effective payment reduction.

In practice, the Fed's 2001 rate cuts (from 6.5% to 1%) did boost housing, but the effect was delayed and amplified by other factors (loose lending standards, securitization). The 2007-2009 rate cuts (from 5.25% to 0%) had a much smaller effect on housing because of tight credit and negative equity.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 5.30%. b) Initial payment = \$1,898, new payment = \$1,669. c) 12.1% reduction. d) 12.1% increase in sales. e) Credit tightening, uncertainty, negative equity, supply constraints, short-run stickiness, other costs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying pass-through coefficients and elasticity estimates to quantify the effect of expansionary rate cuts on mortgage payments and housing demand.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing the Effectiveness of QE on Long-Term Interest Rates

image.alt: Table: QE1 ($1.75T) → 10-year yield change -1.2%; QE2 ($0.6T) → -0.3%; QE3 ($0.85T) → -0.2%; diminishing returns shown.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.11: Worked example: estimating the marginal effect of QE on long-term Treasury yields.

worked_examples.problem:
A central bank is considering QE to combat a recession with the policy rate already at zero. Research suggests that the marginal effect of QE on the 10-year government bond yield is: $\Delta i = -0.6 \times (\text{QE size as % of GDP})$ for the first 10% of GDP, with diminishing returns thereafter (elasticity decays exponentially).

GDP = \$25 trillion.

a) Calculate the expected reduction in the 10-year yield for QE of \$1 trillion (4% of GDP).
b) Calculate the expected reduction for an additional \$1 trillion (total \$2 trillion, 8% of GDP).
c) Calculate the expected reduction for an additional \$1 trillion (total \$3 trillion, 12% of GDP), assuming the effect decays by 50% for purchases beyond 10% of GDP.
d) If the 10-year yield is initially 3.0%, what is the yield after \$3 trillion QE?
e) Should the central bank do \$3 trillion QE or stop at \$2 trillion? Discuss trade-offs.

step_by_step.step: Calculate effect of first $1 trillion QE
step_by_step.explanation:
QE as % of GDP = $1T / $25T = 0.04 = 4%

Effect = -0.6 × 4% = -2.4 percentage points

Note: This is a large effect, reflecting the high elasticity for the first purchases. In reality, estimates are smaller (0.5-1.0 percentage points for QE1). We'll use the model as specified.

step_by_step.step: Calculate effect of second $1 trillion QE
step_by_step.explanation:
Cumulative QE = 8% of GDP. Using the same linear model (before diminishing returns):

Effect = -0.6 × 8% = -4.8 percentage points total.

Marginal effect of second trillion = -4.8% - (-2.4%) = -2.4 percentage points

Still linear, no diminishing returns yet (since 8% < 10% threshold).

step_by_step.step: Calculate effect of third $1 trillion QE (diminishing returns)
step_by_step.explanation:
The first 10% of GDP (\$2.5T) has full effect. Third trillion pushes cumulative to 12% of GDP, with the portion beyond 10% (2% of GDP) subject to 50% decay.

Full effect for first 10%: -0.6 × 10% = -6.0 percentage points.

For the additional 2% (beyond 10%), effect = -0.6 × 2% × 0.5 = -0.6 × 0.01 = -0.6 percentage points? Let's recalc carefully:

Additional QE beyond 10% = 2% of GDP. At 50% decay, the coefficient becomes -0.3 (half of -0.6).

Marginal effect of third trillion = -0.3 × 2% = -0.6 percentage points

Total effect after \$3T QE = -6.0% + (-0.6%) = -6.6 percentage points

Initial yield = 3.0%

Change = -6.6 percentage points

Final yield = 3.0% - 6.6% = -3.6%

This is impossible (nominal yields cannot be negative? Actually they can, as seen in Europe and Japan, but -3.6% is extreme). The model is unrealistic for large QE. In reality, yields have a lower bound around -0.5% to -1.0% due to currency storage costs.

The central bank must weigh benefits and costs:

Benefits of additional QE:
• Lower long-term rates (additional -0.6 percentage points from \$2T to \$3T)
• Additional stimulus to housing and investment
• Signal of commitment to easy policy

Costs of additional QE:
1. Diminishing returns: The marginal effect of the third trillion (0.6%) is much smaller than the first trillion (2.4%).
2. Exit difficulty: A larger balance sheet is harder to unwind; tapering may cause market disruption.
3. Inflation risk: Too much QE could overheat the economy, especially if combined with fiscal stimulus.
4. Financial stability: Very low rates encourage risk-taking and asset bubbles.
5. Negative yields: Pushing yields too negative could harm money market funds, banks, and pension funds.

Decision: If the economy is in a deep recession (output gap -5%, deflation risk), the additional stimulus from the third trillion may be worth the costs. If the economy is recovering, the central bank should stop at \$2T. In practice, the Fed stopped QE3 in 2014 after \$1.7T (not \$3T) because the economy was improving and the marginal benefits were small.

The key lesson: QE has diminishing returns. Central banks should do enough to lower long-term rates significantly, but recognize that very large QE programs have high costs and low marginal benefits.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) -2.4 percentage points. b) -2.4 percentage points (marginal). c) -0.6 percentage points (marginal). d) -3.6% (impossible, shows model limits). e) Stop at $2T unless recession severe; diminishing returns make third trillion less cost-effective.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying QE effectiveness estimates to quantify marginal effects and evaluate the trade-offs of large-scale asset purchase programs.

process_flow.process: FOMC identifies recession (falling GDP, rising unemployment, below-target inflation)
process_flow.description: The Fed monitors economic data to detect a downturn and determine the need for expansionary policy.
process_flow.mechanism: Indicators: negative GDP growth, rising unemployment (Sahm rule), falling inflation, widening output gap, deteriorating credit conditions.

image.alt: Diagram: GDP, unemployment, inflation data → output gap calculation → recession signal → FOMC meeting.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.12: Step 1 - The Fed identifies a recession through economic data and decides to ease policy.

process_flow.process: Fed lowers federal funds rate target (conventional easing)
process_flow.description: The FOMC announces a lower target range for the federal funds rate, typically in 25-50 basis point increments.
process_flow.mechanism: Fed uses IORB and ON RRP to implement the new target. Rate cuts reduce borrowing costs throughout the economy.

image.alt: Diagram: FOMC target range lowered → IORB/ON RRP reduced → fed funds rate falls → borrowing costs fall.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.13: Step 2 - The Fed lowers the federal funds rate target, reducing short-term borrowing costs.

process_flow.process: If rates reach zero, Fed implements QE and forward guidance (unconventional easing)
process_flow.description: When the policy rate hits the zero lower bound, the Fed uses QE to lower long-term rates and forward guidance to manage expectations.
process_flow.mechanism: Fed buys long-term bonds (Treasuries, MBS), expanding its balance sheet. QE lowers term premiums. Forward guidance commits to low rates for an extended period.

image.alt: Diagram: ZLB reached → QE announced (bond purchases) → long-term yields fall → forward guidance → expected future rates fall.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.14: Step 3 - At the zero lower bound, the Fed uses QE and forward guidance to provide additional stimulus.

process_flow.process: Lower rates and QE transmit to the real economy
process_flow.description: Changes in interest rates affect investment, consumption, net exports, and asset prices through multiple channels.
process_flow.mechanism: Interest rate channel, credit channel, asset price channel, exchange rate channel, expectations channel all work together to boost aggregate demand.

image.alt: Diagram: Lower rates → lower borrowing costs, higher asset prices, weaker currency → higher investment, consumption, net exports → higher AD.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.15: Step 4 - Expansionary policy transmits to the real economy through multiple channels.

process_flow.process: Higher aggregate demand closes output gap, returns inflation to target
process_flow.description: Increased spending raises GDP, reduces unemployment, and pushes inflation back toward the target.
process_flow.mechanism: As the economy recovers, the output gap narrows, inflation rises, and the Fed monitors progress to determine when to begin exiting expansionary policy.

image.alt: Diagram: AD increase → output gap closes → unemployment falls → inflation rises → Fed monitors for exit timing.
image.caption: Figure 8.3.16: Step 5 - Higher aggregate demand closes the output gap and returns inflation to target, signaling eventual policy normalization.

image.alt: Master framework: Recession identification → conventional rate cuts → if ZLB, QE/forward guidance → transmission to economy → recovery → exit (taper, QT).
image.caption: Figure 8.3.17: Complete framework for expansionary monetary policy: from recession identification to recovery and exit.

summary: Expansionary monetary policy is used to combat recessions by lowering interest rates, increasing the money supply, and stimulating aggregate demand. Conventional tools include lowering the policy interest rate (federal funds rate), open market purchases, and forward guidance. When the policy rate hits the zero lower bound, the Fed uses unconventional tools: quantitative easing (large-scale purchases of long-term bonds), credit easing (purchases of private assets), and enhanced forward guidance. Expansionary policy transmits to the economy through multiple channels: the interest rate channel (lower borrowing costs), credit channel (easier lending standards), asset price channel (higher stock and real estate prices, wealth effect), exchange rate channel (currency depreciation, higher net exports), and expectations channel (lower expected future rates). The 2008 crisis and 2020 pandemic demonstrated the effectiveness of aggressive expansionary policy, but also its risks: inflation (2021-2022), asset bubbles, financial instability, and exit challenges (taper tantrum 2013). The effectiveness of expansionary policy diminishes at the zero lower bound; unconventional tools have diminishing returns. Coordination with fiscal policy enhances effectiveness, especially in deep recessions. Understanding expansionary policy is essential for analyzing how central banks respond to downturns, evaluating policy trade-offs, and anticipating the timing and magnitude of economic recoveries.
